file_name: BABCOCK_WILCOXENTERPRISES,INC_08_04_2015-EX-10.17-INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY AGREEMENT between THE BABCOCK _ WILCOX COMPANY and BABCOCK _ WILCOX ENTERPRISES, INC..txt

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees to reasonably make available to the other Party (i) inventors and other reasonably necessary persons employed by it for the other Party's reasonable needs regarding execution of documents, interviews, declarations and testimony, and (ii) documents, materials and information for the other Party's reasonable good faith needs regarding such IP Proceedings.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party will endeavor to make available to the other Party (i) inventors and other persons as it deems fit for the other Party's needs regarding execution of documents, interviews, declarations, and testimony, and (ii) documents, materials, and information as it sees appropriate for the other Party's needs regarding such IP Proceedings.
perturbation.explanation: The original text includes 'agrees to reasonably make available,' implying a defined standard of cooperation. The change to 'will endeavor to make available' weakens the obligation. Additionally, 'reasonably necessary persons' becomes 'as it deems fit,' and 'reasonable good faith needs' becomes 'as it sees appropriate,' making the availability of personnel and documents entirely discretionary. This introduces ambiguity, potentially undermining the required level of assistance for IP Proceedings.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 28 U.S. Code § 1782 - Assistance to foreign and international tribunals and to litigants before such tribunals
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification introduces ambiguity and undermines the obligation to provide assistance, potentially conflicting with 28 U.S. Code § 1782, which governs assistance to foreign and international tribunals. The statute outlines procedures for U.S. district courts to provide assistance, and the ambiguous language may be interpreted as not meeting the required 'reasonable' effort to comply with requests for evidence for use in foreign tribunals, as compliance becomes discretionary.

Assistance to foreign and international tribunals and to litigants before such tribunals (a) The district court of the district in which a person resides or is found may order him to give his testimony or statement or to produce a document or other thing for use in a proceeding in a foreign or internat
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1782 - Assistance to foreign and international tribunals and to litigants before such tribunals | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1782 - Assistance to foreign and international tribunals and to litigants before such tribunals U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The district court of the district in which a person resides or is found may order him to give his testimony or statement or to produce a document or other thing for use in a proceeding in a foreign or international tribunal, including criminal investigations conducted before formal accusation. The order may be made pursuant to a letter rogatory issued, or request made, by a foreign or international tribunal or upon the application of any interested person and may direct that the testimony or statement be given, or the document or other thing be produced, before a person appointed by the court. By virtue of his appointment, the person appointed has power to administer any necessary oath and take the testimony or statement. The order may prescribe the practice and procedure, which may be in whole or part the practice and procedure of the foreign country or the international tribunal, for taking the testimony or statement or producing the document or other thing. To the extent that the order does not prescribe otherwise, the testimony or statement shall be taken, and the document or other thing produced, in accordance with the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure. A person may not be compelled to give his testimony or statement or to produce a document or other thing in violation of any legally applicable privilege. (b) This chapter does not preclude a person within the United States from voluntarily giving his testimony or statement, or producing a document or other thing, for use in a proceeding in a foreign or international tribunal before any person and in any manner acceptable to him. (June
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation to provide assistance in IP proceedings, potentially conflicting with 28 U.S. Code § 1782. The original text, 'agrees to reasonably make available,' sets a standard. The changed text, 'will endeavor to make available,' introduces ambiguity and discretion, making compliance questionable. 28 U.S. Code § 1782 addresses assistance to foreign and international tribunals, and the ambiguous language in the modified text may be interpreted as not meeting the required effort to comply with requests for evidence, undermining the statute's intent.

perturbation.original_text: SpinCo will, and will cause the applicable members of the SpinCo Group, to take all actions necessary to discontinue the maintenance and prosecution of the Intellectual Property set forth on Schedule 2.6 as of the date any applicable renewal fees are due in the future.
perturbation.changed_text: SpinCo will consider whether to take actions to discontinue the maintenance and prosecution of the Intellectual Property set forth on Schedule 2.6 when any applicable renewal fees are due in the future.
perturbation.explanation: The original text imposes a clear obligation on SpinCo to take all necessary actions to discontinue the maintenance of certain IP. The modified text changes this to a discretionary action, replacing 'will take all actions necessary' with 'will consider whether to take actions,' making the obligation non-mandatory and unenforceable. This introduces ambiguity as to whether SpinCo is actually required to discontinue maintenance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 35 U.S. Code § 41(b) - Patent fees; payment; late payment; surcharge
perturbation.law_explanation: 35 U.S. Code § 41(b) outlines the requirements and timelines for paying patent maintenance fees to keep a patent enforceable. By making the decision to discontinue maintenance discretionary, the modified text introduces the possibility that SpinCo might fail to discontinue maintenance as agreed, which may lead to legal disputes, and potentially contradict agreements made based on the assumption that those patents would lapse due to non-payment of fees. The explicit intent of the original clause to discontinue prosecution directly correlates with allowing patents to lapse through non-payment, a factor influenced by this section of the law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 35 U.S. Code § 41 - Patent fees; patent and trademark search systems | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 41 - Patent fees; patent and trademark search systems U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) General Fees.— The Director shall charge the following fees: (1) Filing and basic national fees.— (A) On filing each application for an original patent, except for design, plant, or provisional applications, $330. (B) On filing each application for an original design patent, $220. (C) On filing each application for an original plant patent, $220. (D) On filing each provisional application for an original patent, $220. (E) On filing each application for the reissue of a patent, $330. (F) The basic national fee for each international application filed under the treaty defined in section 351(a) entering the national stage under section 371, $330. (G) In addition, excluding any sequence listing or computer program listing filed in an electronic medium as prescribed by the Director, for any application the specification and drawings of which exceed 100 sheets of paper (or equivalent as prescribed by the Director if filed in an electronic medium), $270 for each additional 50 sheets of paper (or equivalent as prescribed by the Director if filed in an electronic medium) or fraction thereof. (2) Excess claims fees.— (A) In general.— In addition to the fee specified in paragraph (1)— (i) on filing or on presentation at any other time, $220 for each claim in independent form in excess of 3; (ii) on filing or on presentation at any other time, $52 for each claim (whether dependent or independent) in excess of 20; and (iii) for each application containing a multiple dependent claim, $390. (B) Multiple dependent claims.— For the purpose of computing fees under subparagraph (A), a multiple dependent claim referred to in section 112 or any claim dependi
perturbation.justification: The original contract text creates a clear obligation for SpinCo to discontinue maintenance of intellectual property. The changed text makes this action discretionary ('will consider whether to take actions'), which undermines the initial obligation. While the provided law snippets (35 U.S. Code § 41) primarily address patent fees, the Law Explanation highlights that discretionary maintenance decisions can lead to disputes and potentially contradict agreements relying on patents lapsing due to non-payment. The change from a mandatory action to a discretionary one directly weakens the enforceability of the original intent to discontinue prosecution, creating ambiguity that could lead to a failure to discontinue maintenance as initially agreed, thus creating a legal contradiction as an Ambiguous Legal Obligation.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party will use reasonable efforts to maintain confidentiality of any trade secret or proprietary information contained within the Shared Library Materials in a manner materially consistent with the how the applicable Party protects its own trade secret or proprietary information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party will endeavor, as it sees fit, to maintain confidentiality of any trade secret or proprietary information contained within the Shared Library Materials.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates 'reasonable efforts' to maintain confidentiality, setting a standard of care. The modified text replaces this with 'will endeavor, as it sees fit,' making the level of effort entirely discretionary and subjective. This removes any objective standard for confidentiality, creating ambiguity and weakening the protection of trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Protection of trade secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S. Code § 1836, part of the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), establishes a federal cause of action for trade secret misappropriation. By reducing the obligation to maintain confidentiality to a discretionary action, the modified text increases the risk that a party might not take adequate measures to protect trade secrets within the Shared Library Materials. This could lead to misappropriation, and the ambiguous language may weaken the ability to enforce the agreement under the DTSA because there is no clear, objective standard for what constitutes adequate confidentiality measures.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality of trade secrets by changing 'reasonable efforts' to 'as it sees fit'. This introduces a subjective standard where the original text had an objective one. According to the Law Explanation, this weakening increases the risk of misappropriation and weakens the ability to enforce the agreement under the DTSA. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 18 U.S. Code § 1836, concerns civil proceedings related to trade secret misappropriation, indicating that a standard of care is expected. The shift to a purely discretionary standard contradicts the intent of protecting trade secrets as envisioned by the DTSA.
